one_line_takeaway: A preliminary IntelliJ plugin prototype uses cheap structural code metrics (CC/LOC/LCOM) to flag security-critical methods and an LLM (GPT-5) to generate prevention-oriented explanations in-editor, but on Spring-PetClinic the metrics recall known-vulnerable methods into broad bins without ranking them highly, and LLM explanations remain generic — motivating future security-aware metrics rather than delivering a validated detection system.
key_finding: Metric computation across all 99 methods took about 2 seconds on average; LLM explanation generation averaged approximately 7.2 seconds per method with GPT-5 versus approximately 1.3 seconds with GPT-4o. Criticality-level distributions differed sharply by metric: LOC flagged 35 High / 55 Medium / 9 Low; CC flagged 5 High / 13 Medium / 81 Low; LCOM flagged 23 High / 26 Medium / 21 Low. Against the 8 known-vulnerable ground-truth methods (Table 1), LOC flagged all 8 as High or Medium, CC flagged 3 as High or Medium, and LCOM flagged only 1 as Medium. However, ranking quality was weak: using LOC and CC, only 2 of the 8 vulnerable methods appeared in the project's top-10 most-critical ranks, indicating the metrics recall known-vulnerable methods into broad High/Medium bins but do not rank them near the top. GPT-4o produced faster but more generic explanations; GPT-5 produced more precise, context-aware explanations at higher latency, though still sometimes generic and without pinpointing exact code locations/variables. The plugin was also informally tested on a Kotlin port of the vulnerable Spring-PetClinic app (ref [10]) and showed similar behavior, suggested (not rigorously evaluated) as cross-language potential.
core_contribution: The paper presents a prototype IntelliJ IDEA plugin ('critical-marker-plugin') that shifts vulnerability handling from post-hoc detection to proactive prevention. It has two stages: (1) a fast, deterministic security-criticality assessment of Java methods using lightweight code-level software metrics (cyclomatic complexity CC, lines of code LOC, and lack of cohesion of methods LCOM, computed via the CK tool), with methods binned into High/Medium/Low criticality via quantile-based equal-frequency binning; and (2) LLM-based explanation generation, where a zero-shot role-playing prompt (system + user prompt) feeds the flagged method's body and metric value/interpretation to an LLM (primarily GPT-5, gpt-5-2025-08-07, plus GPT-4o, gpt-4o-2024-08-06, via Azure OpenAI) to produce a prevention-focused explanation and precautionary steps, displayed directly in the editor via gutter icons/annotations and tooltips. This is the authors' own system contribution, distinct from cited prior work such as SAFE (Johnson et al. 2025, ref [8]), which the paper explicitly frames as explaining already-detected vulnerabilities post hoc, whereas this paper's own goal is proactive explanation of security-critical code before a vulnerability is introduced.
threat_model: Not an adversarial threat model in the classic sense; the paper targets unintentional vulnerability introduction by developers who lack security expertise, arising from code complexity and insufficient understanding of security-critical operations (e.g., data access, authentication, input handling). The 'defender' is the developer using the IDE plugin; there is no adversary model for attacking the plugin/LLM itself, and the paper does not address prompt injection or adversarial code inputs to the LLM component.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: (1) structural software metrics (CC, LOC, LCOM) are indirect proxies for security relevance, not semantic security measures, so they produce false positives (non-critical methods flagged as critical) and do not reliably rank truly vulnerable methods highly (only 2/8 in top-10 for LOC and CC); (2) LLM explanations, even from GPT-5, remain overly generic and do not pinpoint exact vulnerable code locations or variables, a limitation attributed to the underlying metrics lacking security semantics and to GPT-5's general-purpose training; (3) the evaluation is a single-application preliminary study (Spring-PetClinic, 99 methods) with a small ground-truth set of 8 vulnerable methods drawn from the authors' own earlier tooling papers, not an independent or large-scale benchmark; (4) benchmarking across LLMs was explicitly out of scope; (5) no evaluation of LLM hallucination, reproducibility, or adversarial robustness of the explanation stage was performed — the paper defers this to future work (proposed guardrails resembling AWS Automated Reasoning Checks).

datasets_used: Kotlin port of the vulnerable Spring-PetClinic app (informal secondary test) [ref 10]
quantitative_results: Metric computation for all 99 methods: ~2 seconds on average
Explanation generation latency: ~7.2 seconds/method with GPT-5 (gpt-5-2025-08-07); ~1.3 seconds/method with GPT-4o (gpt-4o-2024-08-06)
Criticality distribution via LOC: 35 High, 55 Medium, 9 Low
Criticality distribution via CC: 5 High, 13 Medium, 81 Low
Criticality distribution via LCOM: 23 High, 26 Medium, 21 Low
Of 8 known-vulnerable ground-truth methods: LOC flagged all 8 as High/Medium; CC flagged 3 as High/Medium; LCOM flagged 1 as Medium
Using LOC and CC, only 2 of 8 vulnerable methods appeared among the project's top-10 most-critical ranked methods
baselines_compared: No external baseline tool comparison; comparison is between the paper's own three metrics (CC vs LOC vs LCOM) and between two LLMs (GPT-5 vs GPT-4o) for explanation quality/latency
Ground truth for vulnerable methods taken from authors' own prior work: Krishnamurthy et al. 2022 (Kotlin/Java taint analysis study, ref 10) and Piskachev et al. 2021 SecuCheck (ref 12)
